title: Data Oriented Runtime Adaptation

strong: data oriented pipeline for hosted runtime

use_for: adapting data-oriented runtime concepts to a constrained host environment
choosing which pipeline, storage, scheduler, phase, and diagnostics ideas to keep or simplify
avoiding over-importing a general runtime into a smaller hosted system
designing deterministic update phases around data ownership
do_not_use_for: generic object-oriented module design without data pipeline needs
rules: adapt concepts by runtime constraint, not by source implementation shape
keep phase, ownership, and diagnostics ideas before importing scheduler complexity
prefer simple serial execution until measured workload justifies parallelism
make data ownership and write boundaries explicit before optimizing storage
separate host lifecycle from pipeline phase execution
procedure: Define host constraints: thread access, frame timing, lifecycle instability, allocation budget, and external mutation rules.
Choose required runtime concepts: phases, modules, storage, scheduler, command queue, diagnostics, or replay.
Map domain data to stable snapshots, transient state, published state, and command outputs.
Define phase order and write ownership for each data set.
Select the simplest execution model that satisfies determinism and workload needs.
Add diagnostics for phase timing, dropped work, invalid state, and ownership violations.

primary_when: the task is primarily about adapting data-oriented runtime ideas to a hosted application or mod
must_not_own_when: the task only needs ordinary service composition
the task is purely about UI layout

solves: how to use data-oriented pipeline ideas without importing unnecessary runtime complexity
how to choose phase, storage, scheduler, and diagnostics boundaries for a constrained host
how to keep deterministic data updates under lifecycle constraints

core_principles: A data-oriented runtime is a set of ownership and execution rules before it is a scheduler.
Hosted environments often need stricter external mutation boundaries than standalone runtimes.
Parallelism is an optimization layer; deterministic phase design comes first.
Published state should be stable enough for UI, diagnostics, and replay consumers.
constraints: Do not copy a general runtime wholesale when a smaller phase model is enough.
Do not introduce worker execution for host APIs that require main-thread access.
Do not optimize storage layout before data ownership is understood.
Do not merge transient calculation state with published state without a reason.
decision_rules: If data changes every frame and drives multiple consumers, model it as pipeline state.
If host objects are unstable, snapshot them before pipeline execution.
If output mutates external state, publish commands and apply them at the host boundary.
If a phase has no independent workload, keep it serial and simple.
If parallel execution is considered, define write ownership and diagnostics first.

avoid_patterns: host API calls inside worker tasks
copying runtime class names as architecture

recipes.name: Adapt a pipeline to a hosted runtime
recipes.when: Use when data-oriented concepts are useful but the host imposes lifecycle and threading limits.
steps: List host limits and main-thread-only operations.
Define phase order around snapshot, compute, publish, and apply.
Assign data ownership and write permissions per phase.
Choose serial execution first unless measured workload requires more.
Expose phase timing and ownership diagnostics.

pitfalls: Importing scheduler complexity before the domain needs it.
Letting pipeline stages access live host objects directly.
Conflating transient compute data with public state.
Treating data-oriented design as a naming style rather than ownership discipline.
review_checklist: Are phase boundaries and write ownership clear?
Are diagnostics sufficient to inspect phase behavior?

references.title: Pipeline Architecture
references.interpretation: Use for phase and pipeline ownership before adapting runtime complexity.
references.why: Adaptation should preserve pipeline invariants without copying implementation shape.

references.title: Execution Model
references.interpretation: Use when deciding between serial, scheduled, or parallel execution.
references.why: Execution complexity must follow workload and ownership needs.
